context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on the spider diagram assignment for my city case study. Can you just explain the whole thing from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you get oriented! Before I dive in, let me ask: have you already picked your case study city, and have you gone through the course material on the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation? Knowing where you're starting from will help me point you in the right direction.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full explanation, which would be too much scaffolding and would not involve them in the discovery process. Instead, I should ask a targeted question to find out where exactly they are stuck, so I can provide bite-sized, targeted guidance. I need to avoid giving a full walkthrough unprompted.

exchanges.student: I picked a city, but I still feel confused about basically all of it. I looked at the pathways, but when I try to do the diagram I don’t know what I’m supposed to put where.
exchanges.tutor: Good, you have a city — that's the foundation! Let's narrow down the confusion. Can you name the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation as you understand them from the course? Even a rough attempt is fine. That'll help us figure out where the gap is.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a city but is unsure how to populate the diagram. Rather than explaining the whole structure, I should ask them to tell me what they understand about the 5 Pathways so far, which will reveal their actual gap and let me use guided discovery from there. Bite-sized, Socratic approach.

exchanges.student: I honestly can’t remember them clearly, and that’s part of why I’m stuck. Could you maybe just walk me through what the spider diagram is asking for overall, because I think I’m confused about the assignment and the pathways at the same time?
exchanges.tutor: That makes sense — let's untangle it. The pathways are core course content, so I'd encourage you to go back to that section of the course and jot them down before we continue. Once you have them in front of you, the diagram will make a lot more sense. Can you do that and come back with the list?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is conflating two sources of confusion: the assignment structure and the course content (the 5 Pathways). I should separate these two issues and address them one at a time. I'll direct them back to the course material for the pathways, since that's content they need to review themselves. Then I can help them understand what the diagram asks for at a high level without giving away the structure or doing the work for them. Keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I can, but I’m still not sure what I’m supposed to do with that list once I have it. Like is the assignment asking me to summarize policies, list actors, rate the city somehow, or just collect examples?
exchanges.tutor: Good question to be asking! Look at the assignment description again — it says you're mapping out 'climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways.' What do you think 'climate action' might look like in a city? What kinds of things would you be looking for?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a good clarifying question about what the diagram requires. I can point them to the assignment description without giving the full answer. The assignment says to 'map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways,' which suggests connecting city-specific actions to each pathway. I should nudge them toward that understanding through a question rather than explaining it outright.

exchanges.student: Maybe like plans or projects or policies? But then I start overthinking it and feel like it could also be data, organizations, decision-making, risks... so then it feels like everything counts and I don’t know how detailed I’m supposed to be.
exchanges.tutor: You're actually thinking along the right lines — plans, projects, policies, actors are all relevant. Here's the thing: the 5 Pathways will naturally tell you what to look for under each 'spoke' of the diagram. That's exactly why reviewing them first is the key move. Go look them up, list them, and then ask yourself: what is my city doing that connects to each one? Start there and come back with what you find.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is actually thinking quite well and has identified many relevant elements. Rather than validating everything equally or narrowing it down for them, I should help them realize that the assignment has a specific structure (the spider diagram with 5 pathways) that naturally organizes and limits what they need to find. I want to redirect them to go get the pathways first, since that structure will answer their 'what counts' question organically. I'll affirm their thinking briefly and push them toward the next concrete step.

exchanges.student: Okay, but that still feels really abstract to me. Could you just give me a simple plain-English overview of how the spider diagram is supposed to work, without going city-specific?
exchanges.tutor: Fair enough. A spider diagram has a center (your city) and 'spokes' radiating out — each spoke represents one of the 5 Pathways. Your job is to find evidence of your city's actions along each spoke. That's the structure. The content is what you need to research. So the real next step is still the same: go get those 5 Pathways and we can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is pushing for a general explanation of the spider diagram structure. I can provide a very brief, high-level conceptual description of what a spider diagram is without doing the assignment for them or giving away how to fill it in. This is a reasonable clarification about the assignment format, not the content. I'll keep it brief and then redirect them to take the next step.

exchanges.student: I think I get the shape, but I’m still fuzzy on what counts as “evidence of action.” Is it enough if I found like one climate plan document, or do I need multiple examples under each pathway?
exchanges.tutor: That's really a question for your instructor, since they'll be reviewing the submissions. What I'd ask you to think about though: does one document fully capture what your city is doing along a given pathway, or might there be more to the picture? What does your instinct tell you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a logistical/grading question about how much evidence is needed. This is something the instructor would determine, not me. I should redirect them to the course content/instructor for specifics on depth, but I can encourage them to think about what would make a thorough vs. thin response. I should not answer for the instructor.

exchanges.student: My instinct is probably more than one thing, but then I worry I’m making it too big and doing the wrong kind of detail. I’m kind of wishing someone would just explain the assignment expectations in a straightforward way.
exchanges.tutor: I hear the frustration — scope anxiety is real. But for specific expectations, please reach out to your instructor directly. What I'll say is: don't let perfect be the enemy of started. You have a city, you understand the shape of the diagram. Go look up the 5 Pathways, pick one, and find one example of your city acting on it. That first move will make everything feel less abstract. Want to try that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing frustration and anxiety about scope. This is a logistical question about grading expectations that genuinely belongs to the instructor. I should be empathetic, redirect them to the instructor for that specific question, but also gently push them to just start rather than waiting for perfect clarity. I should not let this circular worry continue — it's time to encourage action.

exchanges.student: I guess, but I still feel lost enough that I don’t know if I’d even recognize a good example when I see one. Could you at least explain what kind of information usually belongs in each part of the spider diagram table version?
exchanges.tutor: I think the best way to recognize a good example is to try finding one and then we can discuss it together. You've been thinking about this for a while — let's shift gears. Go look up the 5 Pathways in the course, pick the one that resonates most with your city, find something your city is doing related to it, and bring it here. I can help you think through whether it fits once there's something concrete on the table.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is going in circles and asking me to essentially pre-explain the assignment content for them. This is the third or fourth time they're asking for a walkthrough before trying anything. I should be firm but supportive, and push them to make a first attempt. I should not provide a template or content guide. I need to redirect them to take action — look up the pathways, pick one, find something, and bring it back to me so we can work with actual material.

exchanges.student: Okay, I’ll try that, but I’m still pretty unsure. If I come back with something random, I’m not sure I’ll know why it fits or doesn’t fit.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly what I'm here for — bring back whatever you find, even if you're unsure, and we'll reason through it together. 'Wrong' attempts are how learning happens. Go try it!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still hesitating, but this is now slightly different — they're worried about failing before they start. I should be encouraging and brief, reassure them that 'wrong' attempts are part of learning, and make clear I'll help them reason through it when they return. The key is to get them to actually go do the work. I should close this loop and send them off.

deductions.reason: The student repeatedly signals the same confusion — not knowing what counts as evidence, what level of detail is expected, and what belongs in each part of the diagram — across multiple turns. Rather than diagnosing the specific gap (e.g., asking what the student thinks a 'pathway' means, or probing what they already know about their city's climate actions), the tutor repeatedly redirects to 'go look up the 5 Pathways' without checking whether the student has a conceptual block, a research skills gap, or simply needs a concrete example to anchor understanding. The tutor's repeated same-direction nudge without diagnostic questioning misses the student's actual point of confusion.

The tutor consistently redirects the student back to course materials and to the instructor without explaining the pedagogical rationale — e.g., why working through the pathways independently builds the skills needed for the assignment, or why the tutor is not simply summarizing the assignment structure. The student is left feeling frustrated without understanding why the tutor is withholding direct explanation.

The conversation ends without any reflection or transfer support. The tutor never prompts the student to articulate what they now understand differently, what strategy they will use, or why the approach of starting with one pathway and one example is methodologically sound. The closing turn is encouraging but does not reinforce method choices or invite reflection.

The tutor cycles on the same move — 'go look up the 5 Pathways and come back' — across at least five turns without synthesizing, reframing, or meaningfully advancing the student's understanding. The student remains stuck in the same place and the tutor does not switch to a different strategy (e.g., offering a worked analogy, a simpler scaffold, or a targeted simplification of what a pathway is). This constitutes pattern-level repetition without progress.

grade.overview: The tutor avoids doing the assignment for the student and maintains a Socratic stance throughout, which is commendable. However, the conversation reveals significant weaknesses in scaffolding and dialogue progression. The tutor's primary failure is repeating the same redirect ('go look up the 5 Pathways') across five or more turns without diagnosing why the student remains stuck or switching to a different pedagogical strategy. This triggers both the 1.2.B.a deduction (misreading the student's confusion and responding with the same move rather than diagnostic questioning) and the 2.1.A.a deduction (pattern-level repetition without synthesis or advancement). The tutor also does not explain its tutoring rationale to the student, leaving the student frustrated without understanding why the tutor is withholding direct explanation (1.3.A.a). Finally, the conversation ends without any reflection or transfer support (1.3.D.a). Communication quality is strong — responses are concise, clear, and appropriately toned throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: No deductions. The tutor never gives the student submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently avoids stating conclusions before the student reasons. Full 12 points retained. 1.2: The student signals the same confusion repeatedly across turns 3–10 (what counts as evidence, what level of detail, what belongs in each part). The tutor's response is consistently the same redirect without diagnostic questioning to identify the specific gap. This matches 1.2.B.a — tutor responds to the wrong confusion point by jumping to the same directive rather than probing what the student already understands. 3 points deducted. 1.3: The tutor enforces a 'go look it up yourself' constraint without explaining why this approach serves the student's learning (1.3.A.a, 2 points). The conversation ends at turn 10 with no reflection prompt or method reinforcement (1.3.D.a, 2 points). No deduction for 1.3.B because the tutor does give some process-level direction (start with one pathway, find one example). 2.1: The tutor repeats the same move — 'go look up the 5 Pathways' — in turns 3, 5, 6, 8, and 9 without consolidating, reframing, or advancing. The student remains in the same confused state throughout. This is clear pattern-level repetition without progress (2.1.A.a, 4 points). 2.2: No deductions. The tutor keeps the student anchored to the assignment and does not engage with off-topic content. 3.1: No deductions. Responses are appropriately concise and clear. 3.2: No deductions. Tone is warm and supportive throughout; no grading language used.
